front: What is the fundamental operation and worst-case time complexity of Selection Sort?
back: Selection Sort repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion and places it at the beginning. Its worst-case and average-case time complexity is O(n²), where n is the number of items, due to the nested loops required for comparisons.

front: How does Bubble Sort work, and what is its key characteristic regarding the state of the list during execution?
back: Bubble Sort works by repeatedly swapping adjacent elements if they are in the wrong order. This process is repeated until the list is sorted. A key characteristic is that after each complete pass, the largest unsorted element 'bubbles up' to its correct position at the end of the list.

front: What is the main advantage of Selection Sort over other simple sorting algorithms like Bubble Sort?
back: The main advantage of Selection Sort is that it makes O(n) swaps in the worst case. This is a significant improvement over Bubble Sort, which can require up to O(n²) swaps, making Selection Sort more efficient in terms of the number of write operations to memory.

front: Are Selection Sort and Bubble Sort stable sorting algorithms?
back: No, the standard implementation of Selection Sort is not stable because it swaps elements that are far apart, which can change the relative order of equal elements. Bubble Sort can be implemented as a stable algorithm since it only swaps adjacent elements.

front: What is the best-case time complexity for Bubble Sort, and under what condition does it occur?
back: The best-case time complexity for Bubble Sort is O(n). This occurs when the input list is already sorted. An optimized version of the algorithm can detect this condition with a single pass (no swaps were made) and terminate early.
